response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
The gender gap in science refers to the disparities in representation between men and women at all levels of scientific research, education, and employment. I believe it should be addressed because equality in any field strengthens society as a whole. From my personal experience growing up with two children—one boy and one girl—I saw how societal expectations could limit opportunities for girls early on.

Although I only have an upper secondary education myself (working as a delivery driver), teaching English classes at our local community center gives me insight into the challenges faced by young people here in Philadelphia. Witnessing firsthand how students' self-esteem can be boosted or broken by teacher comments makes diversity issues particularly real for me. For instance, when I mentioned that girls shouldn't pursue science because it's hard, one of my female students almost cried during class.

I think we should tackle this through a multi-pronged approach starting with education. Programs encouraging more women into STEM subjects from an early age would help, alongside mentorship opportunities so they don't feel alienated later on in their careers.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The gender gap in science is well-documented across various fields and educational levels. It likely stems from societal biases that discourage women from pursuing technical careers. As someone with personal experience raising children and seeing how stereotypes affect young minds, I've concluded that addressing this requires targeted interventions starting early to counteract harmful assumptions.
